questions.text: I feel that having contingency plans is essential because things rarely go as expected.
I focus on solving problems rather than navigating the social landscape around them.
I rarely procrastinate because I tackle tasks as soon as they're identified.
I hold the view that my low emotional reactivity allows me to see situations clearly.
I notice the subtle difference between someone who is listening and someone who is waiting to talk.
I believe that reducing my own consumption so others can have more is a worthy practice.
I would turn down a promotion if it meant my colleagues would be worse off.
I notice when someone strategically drops a name or credential into conversation.
I often position myself as a facilitator in group settings.
Physical sensations accompany my emotions in a way that makes them impossible to ignore.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the foundational value that makes genuine ethics possible rather than merely fashionable.
I often present my conclusions as settled rather than open for debate.
I find that measuring progress too closely can kill the creative spark.
I think that processing suffering as information rather than as an emotional wave is the key to making decisions I will not regret later.
I believe that moral attention to suffering — the active choice to let it count — is what transforms abstract values into concrete behavior.
I find that I thrive when I give myself permission to focus on what I want.
I naturally claim authority in informal group settings.
My emotional reactions are quick to fire and slow to fade.
